title: Heart Failure Self-Management: Daily Weights, Sodium, and Medication Adherence
subtitle: A 68-year-old man with reduced-ejection-fraction heart failure is stable and ready for discharge after a decompensation. The nurse teaches daily weights, a low-sodium diet, medication adherence, and the warning signs that should prompt an early call.

patient.name: Clyde Bennett
patient.admitDate: Cardiology unit, recovering from an acute heart-failure decompensation (HFrEF). Now euvolemic and stable on guideline-directed therapy. Here for discharge self-management teaching: daily weights, low-sodium diet, medication adherence, and symptom monitoring.

vitals.temp: Dry weight set; daily weights taught
Mild exertional dyspnea; cardiac rehab planned
Weight up 6 lb, dyspnea, crackles — readmission

nursesNote: Clyde Bennett, 68, HFrEF, stable and euvolemic after a decompensation, ready for discharge teaching. Self-management priorities: daily morning weights (same time/scale, after voiding) and report a gain >2-3 lb in a day or 5 lb in a week; low-sodium diet (~2 g/day, avoid canned/processed/restaurant foods) with fluid restriction if ordered; take all medications consistently (diuretic in the morning) and AVOID NSAIDs; monitor for increasing dyspnea, orthopnea, PND, and edema; balance activity with rest and attend cardiac rehab; update vaccines; keep follow-up; and know when to call vs seek emergency care.
providerOrders: Heart-failure discharge teaching: daily weights, low-sodium diet, fluid restriction if ordered
Medication reconciliation; reinforce adherence; avoid NSAIDs
Teach weight-gain thresholds and worsening-symptom warning signs
Refer to cardiac rehabilitation; update influenza/pneumococcal vaccines
Schedule follow-up; teach when to call versus seek emergency care

opts.text: Daily weights detect fluid retention early in heart failure
A gain of 2-3 lb in a day or 5 lb in a week should be reported
A low-sodium diet helps control fluid retention
Increasing dyspnea, orthopnea, and edema signal worsening HF
It is safe to stop HF medications once symptoms improve
NSAIDs can worsen heart failure by causing fluid retention
Weigh at the same time each morning, after voiding, in similar clothing
Take medications consistently; the diuretic is best in the morning
Read labels and avoid canned, processed, and restaurant foods high in sodium
Balance activity with rest; cardiac rehab is beneficial
Doubling up after a missed dose is the safest approach
Report early weight gain or worsening symptoms before a crisis
Teach daily weights and the weight-gain thresholds to report
Review medications, timing, and which drugs (NSAIDs) to avoid
Teach the low-sodium (± fluid-restricted) diet
Review symptom monitoring and when to call vs seek emergency care
Arrange follow-up, cardiac rehab, and vaccines; document

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Does the statement show correct understanding?

opts.text: "I'll weigh myself every morning and call about a 3-pound overnight gain."
"I'll limit salt and avoid canned and processed foods."
"I'll take my medications every day, even when I feel well."
"I'll take my water pill in the morning to avoid being up all night."
"I'll get my flu and pneumonia vaccines."
"I'll keep my follow-up appointments and go to cardiac rehab."
"When I feel fine, I'll skip my diuretic to avoid bathroom trips."
"For aches, I'll take ibuprofen regularly."
"A little extra salt is fine as long as I take my pills."

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm the patient can state the daily-weight routine and report thresholds
Reconcile medications and review avoidance of NSAIDs
Teach label reading and low-sodium meal planning with teach-back
Review red-flag symptoms and the call-vs-emergency action plan
Schedule follow-up/cardiac rehab and document the teaching
Daily weights stay stable with no increasing edema
He breathes comfortably and sleeps flat without extra pillows
He takes all medications, follows a low-sodium diet, and attends rehab
He calls early about a 4-pound gain and the diuretic is adjusted
Weight climbs 6 lb in a week with worsening ankle edema
He develops new orthopnea and wakes breathless at night
He stops the diuretic and becomes short of breath
Regular NSAID use causes fluid retention and a HF flare
